items.stemText: A steel rod 2 m long has a diameter of 20 mm and carries an axial tensile load of 40 kN. Using $E = 200$ GPa for steel, determine the total elongation of the rod.
items.explanationText:
Cross-sectional area: $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi(20)^2}{4} = 314.16$ mm².

Axial stress: $\sigma = P/A = 40{,}000/314.16 = 127.32$ MPa (not asked directly, but useful as a check that the stress is well within the elastic range of steel).

Elongation: $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE} = \dfrac{(40{,}000)(2000)}{(314.16)(200{,}000)} = \dfrac{80{,}000{,}000}{62{,}831{,}853} = 1.27$ mm.

intelligence.engineeringNotes: Working entirely in N and mm gives stress directly in MPa (since 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa) without an extra conversion step — convert kN to N and GPa to MPa (×1000) before substituting, not after.
commonMistakes: Using A = πd² (forgetting the /4), which understates the elongation by a factor of 4
Leaving E in GPa instead of converting to MPa, which overstates the elongation by a factor of 1000
Reporting the stress (127.3 MPa) instead of the elongation that the question actually asks for

items.stemText: A solid circular steel shaft 50 mm in diameter transmits a torque of 1.2 kN·m. Determine the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.

intelligence.engineeringNotes: T = 1.2 kN·m must be converted to N·mm (×1000×1000 = ×1,000,000 → 1,200,000 N·mm) to stay consistent with d in mm and get stress directly in MPa.
commonMistakes: Using r = d = 50 mm instead of r = d/2 = 25 mm, which doubles the computed stress to 97.8 MPa
Forgetting to convert kN·m to N·mm
Using the rectangular-section inertia formula bh³/12 instead of the circular polar formula πd⁴/32

items.stemText: A rectangular timber beam 150 mm wide and 300 mm deep is subjected to a maximum bending moment of 45 kN·m. Determine the maximum bending stress in the beam.
items.explanationText:
Moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{(150)(300)^3}{12} = 337{,}500{,}000$ mm⁴.

Distance to extreme fiber: $c = h/2 = 150$ mm.

Maximum bending stress: $\sigma = \dfrac{Mc}{I} = \dfrac{(45{,}000{,}000)(150)}{337{,}500{,}000} = 20$ MPa.

intelligence.engineeringNotes: For a symmetric rectangular section, c = h/2 because the neutral axis passes through the centroid at mid-depth; this is not true for unsymmetric sections (e.g., a T-section), where the tension- and compression-side c values differ.
commonMistakes: Using c = h (the full depth) instead of c = h/2, which doubles the computed stress to 40 MPa
Swapping b and h in the moment-of-inertia formula (using hb³/12 instead of bh³/12), which gives 80 MPa here
Using I = bh³/3 instead of bh³/12, which understates the stress to 5 MPa
